Training set-up:ActivationFunction
Sigmoid
•Mathematically,itsquashes numbers to range [0,1]•Historically popular:ØRelate the output to a probability score.ØInterprete as a saturating “firing rate” of a biologicalneuron

Training set-up:ActivationFunction
ProblemsforSigmoidFunction1.Saturated neurons “kill” the gradients andweightswillneverchange2.Vanishing Gradient Problem in deep layers

Training set-up:ActivationFunctionProblemsforSigmoidFunction1.Saturated neurons “kill” the gradients,andweightswillneverchange2.Vanishing Gradient Problem in deep layers3.Sigmoid outputs are not zero-centered;thegradientwillbeallpositiveorallnegative(normally slows convergence)
local gradient(>0)
Inputor activation from the previous layer(>0)Weight vectors don't explore both directions effectively.

[LeCunetal.,1991]

Training set-up:ActivationFunction
ReLU(Rectified Linear Unit)
•Computationally efficient•Does not saturate (in +region)•Dead neurons: inactivateReLUunits may neverupdate
f(x) = max(0, x)
[Krizhevskyetal.,2012]
𝑑𝑓𝑑𝑥=-1,𝑖𝑓	𝑥≥00,𝑖𝑓	𝑥<0

25
Training set-up:ActivationFunction
LeakyReLU[Maas et al., 2013] [He et al., 2015]
f(x) = max(0.01x,x)•Does not saturate (in +region)•Very computationally efficient•Converges much faster than sigmoid/tanh in practice•Avoids dead neurons 
𝑑𝑓𝑑𝑥=-1,𝑖𝑓	𝑥≥00.01,𝑖𝑓	𝑥<0

Learnable parameter
Training set-up:ActivationFunction
LeakyReLU[Mass et al., 2013] [He et al., 2015]
f(x) = max(0.01x,x)•Does not saturate (in +region)•Very computationally efficient•Converges much faster than sigmoid/tanh in practice•NeverdieParametric Rectifier (PReLU)f(x) = max(ax,x)

Training set-up:ActivationFunction
[Goodfellow et al., 2013]
Maxout
•Does not have the basic form of “dot product → nonlinearity”•Generalizes ReLU and Leaky ReLU•Linear Regime! Does not saturate! Does not die!•Problem: doubles the number of parameters/neuron

28
Inpractice:•Use ReLUasdefault. •Try out Leaky ReLU/Maxout/ELU.To squeeze out some marginal gainsReflection: what are our main considerations?

This simulates natural variations in lighting and camera settings that occur in real-world environments.

Glorot and Bengio, “Understandingthe difficultyof training deep feedforward neural networks”,AISTAT2010
Q:WhyXavier initializationworks?Let: y=x1w1+x2w2+...+xDinwDinAssume: Var(x1) = Var(x2)= …=Var(xDin)andallxi,wiareiidand zero-centered We want: Var(y) = Var(xi)Var(y)= Var(x1w1+x2w2+...+xDinwDin)
= Din*Var(xiwi)
iid:Independent and identically Distributed random variables
Variance of independent variables :Va r (A+B)=Va r (A)+Va r ( B )
easilyfulfilduringdatapreprocessing

48
Training set-up:WeightInitialization
Glorot and Bengio, “Understandingthe difficultyof training deep feedforward neural networks”,AISTAT2010
Q:WhyXavier initializationworks?Let: y=x1w1+x2w2+...+xDinwDinAssume: Var(x1) = Var(x2)= …=Var(xDin)andall xi, wi are iid and zero-centeredWe want: Var(y) = Var(xi)Var(y)= Var(x1w1+x2w2+...+xDinwDin) = Din*Var(xiwi) 
 = Din*Var(xi)*Var(wi) [E(x)=0, E(w)=0, E(y)=0]Va r (𝑋𝑌)=𝐸[(𝑋2𝑌2)−𝐸(𝑋𝑌)]2=Var(𝑋)Var(𝑌)+Var(𝑋)(𝐸(𝑌))2+Var(𝑌)(𝐸(𝑋))2
Std(wi)=1/sqrt(Din)

Glorot and Bengio, “Understandingthe difficultyof training deep feedforward neural networks”,AISTAT2010
Q:WhyXavier initializationworks?Let: y=x1w1+x2w2+...+xDinwDinAssume: Var(x1) = Var(x2)= …=Var(xDin)We want: Var(y) = Var(xi)Var(y)= Var(x1w1+x2w2+...+xDinwDin) = Din*Var(xiwi) = Din*Var(xi)*Var(wi) 
Std(wi)=1/sqrt(Din)
Din*Var(wi)=1

50
Training set-up:WeightInitialization
ChangefromtanhtoReLU
Xavier assumes zero centeredactivationfunctionActivationscollapsetozero again,nolearning
Xavier works well for activations that are centered around zero (e.g., tanh). But it doesn't work well for functions, which only give positive outputs (e.g.,relu).

51
Training set-up:WeightInitialization
Kaiming Initialization:std=sqrt(2/Din)Wonderful:Activations are nicely scaled for all layers!
Heet al,“DelvingDeepinto Rectifiers:Surpassing Human-LevelPerformance onImageNet Classification”, ICCV2015

52
Summary•Initializing the weights tosmall random valuesleads to the problem of vanishing gradients. This is because the gradients flowing into a particular neuron are proportional to the activation that it receives. •Xavier initialization works well for activations that are roughly zero-centered, such as tanh. •When usingReLUactivation that does not have zero mean, it’s recommended to use the Kaiming initialization.
Training set-up:WeightInitialization

∇𝑓(𝑥;𝑤1,…,𝑤𝑛)=𝜕𝑓(𝑥)𝜕𝑤1⋮𝜕𝑓(𝑥)𝜕𝑤𝑛
Learningrateorstepsize

54
Training set-up:LearningrateLarge learning rateØSpeeds up early trainingØHelps escape flat or bad local minimaØRisk: may overshoot or cause instability
Small learning rateØEnables fine-tuning and stable convergenceØBetter for late-stage trainingØRisk: slow learning, stuck in suboptimal solutions

55
Learning Rate Schedulers:Learningratedecay
Reducelearningrateatafewfixed points. E.g., for ResNets, multiply LR by 0.1 after epochs 30, 60, and 90.Steps:ØStart with large LR → faster explorationØGradually reduce LR → smoother convergenceØGoal: balance speed and stability

59
Training set-up:LearningrateLearning rate warm-upHigh initial learning rates can make loss explode; linearly increasing learning rate from 0 over the first ~5,000 iterations can prevent this.Warm-up is especially common when using inverse square root decay or other advanced schedules.
Goyal et al, “Accurate, Large Minibatch SGD: Training ImageNet in 1 Hour”, arXiv 2017

Learning Rate SchedulersAdjusts the learning rate according to a pre-defined schedule during the training process.
https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/recipes/compiling_optimizer_lr_scheduler.html
Can we use optimizers and LR schedulerstogether?

Learning Rate SchedulersAdjusts the learning rate according to a pre-defined schedule during the training process.
https://docs.pytorch.org/tutorials/recipes/compiling_optimizer_lr_scheduler.html
Optimizers adjust the size and direction of updates dynamically for each parameter, based on historical gradient information.Learning rate schedulers adjust the global learning rate over time  based on the training step or epoch.
Can we use optimizers and LR schedulerstogether?

62
Inpractice:•Initializeyourlearningratetoasmallvalue,like10-2,10-3,10-4
•Trydifferentlearningrateschedulerstogetbetterperformance•Aslearningrateiscloselyrelatedtothetrainingset-up(e.g.,batchsize),dataset,modelarchitecture.So,selecttheappropriatetrainingschedulerrequireshugeexperimentalefforts.Therefore,itwillsaveyourtimebyfollowingthesuggestedsettingofstate-of-the-artresearchpapers

WhydoesDropoutwork?Regularization:Dropout
cat score
X
X
hasanearhas a tail is furry hasclawsmischievouslookX
Dropoutforces the network to learn redundant  and distributedrepresentation that aren’t dependent on any single pathway through the network.

70
WhydoesDropoutwork?Regularization:Dropout
Dropout is training a large ensemble of  models (that share parameters).Each binary mask is one modelAn FC layer with 4096 units has  24096 ~ 101233 possible masks!Only ~ 1082 atoms in the universe...

71
AtTrainTime: Drop connections between neurons (set weights to 0)AtTestTime: Use all the connections
Regularization:DropConnect
Wanetal,“RegularizationofNeuralNetworksusingDropConnect”,ICML2013

72
AtTrainTime: Skip some layers in the network AtTestTime: Use all the layers

Huangetal,“DeepNetworkswithStochasticDepth”,ECCV2016
TrainTest

Regularization: ACommonRule
Training:Addsomekind ofrandomness
Testing:Averageoutrandomness (sometimesapproximate)
It prevents the network from becoming too dependent on any particular feature.

78
Step 1: Sanity checkCheck trainloss at initializationtomakesureyournetworkisproperlyinitializede.g., ln(C) for softmax with C classes
Choosing hyperparameters
Tip: L=-ln(P(y=t|x))If the initial loss is wildly different from what you expect, there might be a bug in your data pipeline or initialization!

79
Step 1: Sanity checkStep 2: Tryoverfit ona small trainingdatasubsetTry to train to 100% training accuracy on a small sample of training data (~5-10 minibatches).Check architecture, learning rate, weight initializationtomakesurethewholetrainingpipelineisworking.•Loss not going down? Learningrate too low, bad initialization•Loss explodes to Inf or NaN? Learning rate too high, bad initialization•…
Choosing hyperparameters

80
Step 1: Sanity checkStep 2: Tryoverfit ona small trainingdatasubsetStep3:Find LR that makes loss go down
Choosing hyperparameters
maintain the architecture from the previous step, use all training dataand try to find learning rates that makes the loss drop significantly within ~100 iterationsGood learning rates to try: 10-1, 10-2, 10-3, 10-4Different optimizers work best with different learning rates!

81
Step 1: Sanity checkStep 2: Tryoverfit ona small trainingdatasubsetStep3:Find LR that makes loss go downStep 4: Coarse grid, train for ~1-5 epochsStep 5: Refine grid, train longer  Choose a few values of learning rate and hyperparameters, train a few models for ~1-5 epochs.

Part 2: Multiple-choice Questions (only one correct choice)3. (1 correct choice only) Which of the following is true about the momentum update rule?A. It has no hyperparameters.B. It guarantees convergence to the global optimum.C. If the network has n parameters, momentum requires that we keep track of O(n) extraparameters.D. None of the above.4. B. 16.     C. 17.     D. 32.
